name: Green Chile Chicken Enchiladas (Stacked)
steps: Layer tortillas, chicken, green chile, sauce, and cheese in stacks (New Mexico style, not rolled).
Bake at 375F for 15-18 min until bubbly.
steps_detailed: Preheat the oven to 375F. If your chicken isn't already cooked, poach or bake it now and shred it once cool enough to handle.
Warm the enchilada sauce in a small saucepan over low heat, stirring in the green chile so the flavors meld — about 5 minutes.
Lightly char each tortilla directly over a gas flame or in a dry skillet for 10-15 seconds per side. This isn't just texture — it keeps the tortillas from turning to mush once the sauce hits them.

In a small baking dish, spoon a thin layer of sauce on the bottom. Lay down the first tortilla, then a layer of chicken, a scattering of cheese, and a ladle of sauce. Repeat, finishing with sauce and cheese on top — this is the 'stacked' New Mexico style, not rolled.
Bake uncovered for 15-18 minutes, until the cheese is fully melted and the edges are bubbling. Let it rest 3-4 minutes before serving so the layers set and it doesn't slide apart on the plate.

name: Carne Adovada (Red Chile Braised Pork)
steps: Cook rice per package directions.
Toss pork with red chile powder and garlic, braise covered with 1 cup water 45-50 min until fork-tender.
Serve over rice.
steps_detailed: Pat the pork cubes dry with a paper towel — excess moisture will steam the meat instead of browning it.

Toss the pork with the red chile powder and minced garlic in a bowl, making sure every piece is coated. If you have 20-30 minutes, let it sit at room temperature to let the chile start penetrating the meat.

Heat a heavy pot or Dutch oven over medium-high heat. Sear the pork in batches — don't crowd the pan — until browned on at least two sides, about 2-3 minutes per side. This browning is where most of the flavor comes from, so don't rush it.
Add 1 cup water, scraping up any browned bits from the bottom of the pot. Bring to a simmer, then cover and reduce heat to low.

Braise for 45-50 minutes, checking occasionally that there's still liquid in the pot. The pork is done when it's fork-tender and starting to fall apart — if it still feels firm, give it another 10 minutes.
Taste the sauce and adjust — a pinch more chile powder if you want more heat, a splash of water if it's too thick. Serve over rice, spooning extra sauce over the top.

name: New Mexico Green Chile Stew
steps: Brown pork, add potato, green chile, and broth.
Simmer covered 30-35 min until pork and potato are tender.
steps_detailed: Cut the pork into roughly 1-inch cubes — uniform size means even cooking.
Heat a heavy pot over medium-high heat. Brown the pork in batches, about 2-3 minutes per side, until deeply colored — don't skip this, it's the base of the stew's flavor.
Add the green chile and potato to the pot along with the broth. Stir to combine, scraping up any browned bits stuck to the bottom.

Bring to a boil, then reduce to a gentle simmer and cover. Cook for 30-35 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the pork is tender enough to shred with a fork and the potatoes are soft all the way through.
If the stew looks too thin, uncover for the last 5-10 minutes to let some liquid cook off. If it's too thick, add a splash more broth or water.

name: Posole Rojo
steps: Brown pork, add hominy, red chile powder, and broth.
Simmer covered 35-40 min until pork is tender.
steps_detailed: Cut the pork into 1-inch cubes and pat dry.
Heat a heavy pot over medium-high heat and brown the pork in batches, 2-3 minutes per side, until well-colored on the outside.
Stir in the red chile powder, coating the pork, and cook for 30 seconds until fragrant — this blooms the spice.
Add the hominy and broth, stirring to combine. Bring to a simmer.
Cover and cook on low for 35-40 minutes, until the pork shreds easily with a fork. Stir occasionally and check that it's not sticking to the bottom.
Taste and adjust salt and chile before serving — hominy can mute spice a little, so don't be afraid to add more if it tastes flat.

name: Hatch Chile Rellenos

ingredients.note: or 2 whole roasted hatch chiles if available

steps: Stuff chiles with cheese.
Whisk eggs with flour, dip stuffed chiles, pan-fry 3-4 min per side until golden.
steps_detailed: If using whole hatch chiles, char them over a gas flame or under the broiler, turning until blistered and blackened on all sides, about 8-10 minutes. Place in a bowl, cover with plastic wrap, and let steam for 10 minutes — this loosens the skins.
Peel off the charred skin (it should slip off easily after steaming), then make a small slit down one side and carefully remove the seeds, keeping the chile intact.
Stuff each chile with shredded cheese, pressing gently to pack it in without tearing the pepper.
Whisk the eggs with the flour until smooth and slightly thickened — this batter needs to cling to the chile, not run off.
Heat a lightly oiled pan over medium heat. Dip each stuffed chile in the egg batter, coating fully, and lay it in the pan.
Cook for 3-4 minutes per side, until the batter is golden and set and the cheese inside is melted — resist the urge to flip early, or the batter will tear.

ingredients_full.note: or 3 whole roasted hatch chiles if available

name: New Mexico Green Chile Cheeseburger
steps: Grill or pan-sear patty to desired doneness.
Top with cheese and warmed green chile, serve on a bun.
steps_detailed: Form the ground beef into a patty slightly wider than your bun — it'll shrink as it cooks. Press a shallow dimple into the center with your thumb so it cooks flat instead of doming up.
Season both sides generously with salt just before cooking.
Heat a skillet or grill to medium-high. Cook the patty for 3-4 minutes on the first side without pressing down (pressing squeezes out the juices).
Flip once, top with the green chile and cheese, and cover the pan loosely with a lid or foil for the last 2-3 minutes so the cheese melts fully.
Toast the bun cut-side-down in the same pan for the last minute of cooking to soak up a little flavor. Build the burger and serve immediately.

name: Enchiladas Rojas
steps: Brown beef with red chile powder and a splash of water to form a sauce.
Layer with tortillas and cheese in a stack, bake at 375F for 12-15 min.
steps_detailed: Brown the ground beef in a skillet over medium-high heat, breaking it into small crumbles, about 5-6 minutes.
Stir in the red chile powder and a splash of water, letting it simmer for 2-3 minutes until it forms a thick, coating sauce rather than a dry crumble.
Lightly char each tortilla over a flame or in a dry pan for 10-15 seconds per side to keep them from getting soggy once layered.
In a small baking dish, layer tortilla, beef, and cheese, repeating until you run out, finishing with cheese on top.
Bake at 375F for 12-15 minutes until the cheese is fully melted and just starting to brown at the edges.

name: Carne Adovada Tacos
steps: Warm pork with red chile powder.
Fill warmed tortillas with pork and onion.
steps_detailed: Warm the pulled pork in a skillet over medium heat with the red chile powder, stirring until it's evenly coated and heated through, about 4-5 minutes.
While the pork warms, char the tortillas briefly over a flame or in a dry skillet, about 10 seconds per side, until pliable with a few light char spots.
Fill each tortilla with pork and top with diced onion. Serve immediately while the tortillas are still warm and flexible.

name: Blue Corn Crusted Trout
steps: Dip trout in egg, then blue cornmeal.
Pan-sear 3-4 min per side until crisp and flaky.
steps_detailed: Pat the trout fillet completely dry — any surface moisture will keep the crust from crisping.
Whisk the egg in a shallow dish. Spread the blue cornmeal in a second shallow dish.
Dip the trout in egg, letting excess drip off, then press firmly into the cornmeal so it adheres in an even layer on both sides.
Heat a lightly oiled skillet over medium heat. Lay the trout in skin-side up (if skin-on) and cook undisturbed for 3-4 minutes until the crust is golden and releases easily from the pan.
Flip carefully and cook another 3-4 minutes until the fish flakes easily with a fork. Serve right away — the crust softens quickly as it sits.

name: New Mexico Frito Pie
steps: Brown beef with red chile powder and a splash of water.
Layer over Fritos, top with cheese.
steps_detailed: Brown the ground beef in a skillet over medium-high heat, breaking it into small pieces, about 5-6 minutes.
Stir in the red chile powder and a splash of water, simmering 2-3 minutes until it thickens into a light sauce.
Spread the Fritos in a bowl or directly in the bag if you're going the classic route.
Spoon the beef over the chips while it's still hot, then top with cheese immediately so it melts from the residual heat.

name: Green Chile Chicken Salad Jar
steps: Mix chicken, green chile, and yogurt.
Layer over lettuce in a jar.
steps_detailed: In a bowl, combine the diced chicken, green chile, and yogurt, stirring until evenly coated — taste and add a pinch of salt if needed.
Chop the lettuce and pack it into the bottom of a jar.
Spoon the chicken mixture on top. Seal and refrigerate — this keeps well for 3-4 days.

name: Green Chile Turkey Wrap
steps: Layer turkey, green chile, and cheese on tortilla, roll and slice.
steps_detailed: Lay the tortilla flat and warm it briefly in a dry pan if you'd like it more pliable, about 10 seconds per side.
Layer the turkey slices evenly across the tortilla, leaving a border around the edges.
Spoon the green chile over the turkey and sprinkle the cheese on top.
Fold in the sides, then roll tightly from the bottom, keeping the fillings compact. Slice in half on a diagonal to serve.

name: Calabacitas Bowl
steps: Saute zucchini and corn 6-8 min.
Stir in green chile, top with cheese until melted.
steps_detailed: Heat a splash of oil in a skillet over medium heat. Add the zucchini and cook for 4-5 minutes, stirring occasionally, until it starts to soften and lightly brown at the edges.
Add the corn and cook another 3-4 minutes until heated through and the zucchini is fully tender.
Stir in the green chile and cook 1-2 minutes to warm through.
Sprinkle the cheese over the top, cover, and let sit off heat for a minute or two until melted.

name: Posole and Chicken Jar
steps: Toss chicken and hominy with red chile powder.
Pack with broth on the side to heat and combine.
steps_detailed: Toss the diced chicken and hominy with the red chile powder in a bowl until evenly coated.
Pack the mixture into a jar or container. Pour the broth into a separate small container to keep it from making everything soggy in the fridge.
When ready to eat, combine and microwave until hot, or warm together in a saucepan for a few minutes.

name: New Mexico Breakfast Burrito
steps: Scramble eggs with green chile.
Top with cheese, wrap in tortilla.
steps_detailed: Crack the eggs into a bowl and whisk until no streaks of white remain.
Heat a nonstick skillet over medium heat. Add the eggs along with the green chile.

Let the eggs sit undisturbed for 10-15 seconds until the edges just start to set, then gently push from the edges toward the center with a spatula, tilting the pan to let the uncooked egg flow underneath.
Continue folding gently every 15-20 seconds until just set but still glossy, about 2-3 minutes total — pull off the heat a beat early, since residual heat finishes the cooking.
Warm the tortilla in a dry pan or over a flame for 15-20 seconds per side until pliable.
Sprinkle cheese down the center, top with the eggs, fold in the sides, and roll tightly from the bottom.

name: Blue Corn Pancakes
steps: Whisk cornmeal, egg, and milk to a batter.
Cook on a griddle 2-3 min per side, drizzle with honey.
steps_detailed: Whisk the blue cornmeal, egg, and milk together in a bowl until smooth — the batter will be slightly thinner than a standard pancake batter, which is normal for cornmeal.
Let the batter rest for 5 minutes; this gives the cornmeal a chance to soften slightly and thicken the batter a bit.
Heat a lightly greased griddle or nonstick pan over medium heat. Pour small rounds of batter, about 3 inches across.
Cook until bubbles form across the surface and the edges look dry, about 2-3 minutes, then flip and cook another 1-2 minutes until golden.
Drizzle with honey and serve warm.

ingredients_full.note: melted, plus more for the pan

name: Huevos con Chile

ingredients.note: warmed, or red chile sauce if preferred

steps: Fry eggs.
Serve over warmed tortillas topped with green chile.
steps_detailed: Heat a lightly oiled skillet over medium heat. Crack the eggs in carefully, keeping the yolks intact if you want them fried rather than scrambled.
Cook for 2-3 minutes until the whites are set but the yolks are still soft, or longer if you prefer firmer yolks.
While the eggs cook, warm the green chile in a small pan or the microwave.
Warm the tortillas briefly in a dry skillet, about 10 seconds per side.
Plate the tortillas, top with the eggs, and spoon the warmed green chile over everything.

name: Roasted Hatch Green Chile Dip
steps: Warm all ingredients together until melted and combined.
Serve with vegetable sticks or crackers.
steps_detailed: Combine the green chile, cream cheese, and shredded cheese in a small saucepan over low heat.
Stir frequently as the cheeses melt, about 5-6 minutes, until the mixture is smooth and fully combined — low heat prevents the cheese from separating or going grainy.
Taste and adjust — a pinch of salt or extra chile if you want more kick.
Serve warm with vegetable sticks or crackers for dipping.

name: Pinon Snack Mix
steps: Toss pinon nuts and roasted chickpeas together.
steps_detailed: If your pinon nuts aren't already toasted, warm them in a dry skillet over medium-low heat for 2-3 minutes, shaking the pan often, until fragrant — watch closely, as pine nuts burn quickly.
Let the nuts cool for a minute, then toss them together with the roasted chickpeas in a bowl.
Store in an airtight container if not eating right away — this keeps well for several days at room temperature.

name: Blue Corn Chips with Salsa

ingredients.note: cut into wedges, or blue corn tortilla chips if available

steps: Bake tortilla wedges at 375F for 8-10 min until crisp.
Serve with salsa.
steps_detailed: Preheat the oven to 375F.
Cut the tortillas into wedges and spread them in a single layer on a baking sheet — overlapping pieces won't crisp evenly.
Bake for 8-10 minutes, checking at the 6-minute mark, until the edges curl and turn golden and crisp.
Let cool for a minute or two (they'll crisp up further as they cool) before serving with salsa.

ingredients_full.note: cut into wedges, or blue corn tortilla chips if available

name: Biscochitos
steps: Cream butter and sugar, mix in anise seed and flour to form a dough.
Shape into small cookies, dust with cinnamon sugar, bake at 350F for 10-12 min.
steps_detailed: Cream the softened butter and sugar together in a bowl until light and fluffy, about 2 minutes with a hand mixer or vigorous whisking.
Stir in the anise seed, crushing it slightly between your fingers first to release more flavor as it mixes in.
Gradually mix in the flour until a soft, slightly crumbly dough forms — don't overwork it, or the cookies will turn out tough.
Preheat the oven to 350F. Roll the dough into small balls, about a tablespoon each, and flatten slightly on a baking sheet.
Mix the cinnamon with a little extra sugar and dust generously over the tops.
Bake for 10-12 minutes until the edges are just barely golden — these cookies are meant to stay pale, so don't over-bake. Cool on the sheet for a few minutes before transferring.

name: Sopapillas with Honey
steps: Mix flour, butter, and warm water into a soft dough.
Roll thin, cut into squares, fry until puffed and golden, drizzle with honey.
steps_detailed: Mix the flour and softened butter together with a pinch of salt, working the butter in with your fingers until the mixture looks like coarse crumbs.
Add warm water a tablespoon at a time, mixing until a soft, slightly tacky dough comes together — you may not need all of it.
Knead briefly, just 30 seconds or so, then let the dough rest for 10 minutes covered with a towel.
Roll the dough out thin, about 1/8 inch, and cut into squares roughly 3 inches across.
Heat oil in a deep pan to 375F. Fry the squares a few at a time, turning once, until they puff up and turn golden, about 1-2 minutes per side.
Drain on paper towels and drizzle generously with honey while still warm.

name: Natillas (New Mexican Custard)
steps: Whisk eggs and sugar, slowly whisk in warm milk.
Cook over low heat, stirring constantly, until thickened, about 8-10 min. Dust with cinnamon.
steps_detailed: Whisk the eggs and sugar together in a bowl until pale and slightly thickened.
Warm the milk in a saucepan until it's hot but not boiling — small bubbles forming at the edges is the cue to stop.
Slowly pour about a third of the warm milk into the egg mixture while whisking constantly — this tempers the eggs so they don't scramble.
Pour the tempered egg mixture back into the saucepan with the remaining milk.
Cook over low heat, stirring constantly with a wooden spoon or spatula, until the custard thickens enough to coat the back of the spoon, 8-10 minutes. Don't let it boil, or it will curdle.
Pour into serving dishes, dust with cinnamon, and chill for at least an hour before serving.

name: New Mexico Red Chile Sauce
steps: Whisk chile powder and flour into broth.
Simmer with garlic 10 min, whisking, until thickened.
steps_detailed: Whisk the flour into a small amount of the broth first to form a smooth slurry — this prevents lumps when it hits the rest of the liquid.
In a saucepan, combine the chile powder, garlic, and remaining broth with the slurry, whisking to combine.
Bring to a gentle simmer over medium-low heat, whisking occasionally.
Simmer for 10 minutes, whisking every couple of minutes, until the sauce thickens enough to coat a spoon.
Taste and adjust — a pinch of salt usually rounds it out. Use immediately or store in the fridge for up to a week.

name: Hatch Green Chile Sauce
steps: Simmer green chile and broth 8-10 min.
Whisk in flour, simmer 3-4 more min until thickened, blend if a smoother sauce is preferred.
steps_detailed: Combine the green chile and broth in a saucepan and bring to a simmer over medium heat.
Cook for 8-10 minutes to let the flavors meld and the chile soften further.
Whisk the flour with a splash of cold water to form a smooth slurry, then whisk it into the sauce.
Simmer another 3-4 minutes, whisking occasionally, until thickened.
For a smoother, restaurant-style sauce, blend it briefly with an immersion blender or in a countertop blender before serving.
